content.text: What is Streaming for Latency Reduction?

is a response-delivery technique where the server sends output incrementally as it is generated rather than buffering the complete response before transmitting it. When applied to Large Language Model (LLM) inference, streaming means each generated token is forwarded to the client the moment the model produces it, instead of waiting for the full sequence to complete.

In agentic systems, the performance gap between streaming and blocking is especially pronounced. A typical LLM response might take 8-30 seconds to complete, but the first token is often ready within 50-200 milliseconds. By streaming, the user or downstream system sees output almost immediately — a fundamental shift in perceived responsiveness.
The two dominant transport mechanisms are
— an HTTP/1.1-compatible unidirectional push protocol — and
, which is what most LLM APIs use under the hood. Both deliver a sequence of delta events, each carrying a small slice of the response.
Agentic pipelines chain multiple LLM calls, tool executions, and subagent invocations. In a blocking architecture, end-to-end latency is the

of all individual generation times. In a streaming architecture, the system can begin processing the first step's output while subsequent tokens are still arriving — and can dispatch tools as soon as enough of the response is available, not when all of it is.

Human-facing agents have an especially strong need for streaming: when a user asks a question and waits 20 seconds with no feedback, they assume the system has frozen. Streaming converts a 20-second silence into a typewriter effect that starts within milliseconds — dramatically improving perceived quality even when total generation time is unchanged.

On the infrastructure side, streaming also improves server resource utilization: connections are held open but processing is interleaved, allowing the gateway to pipeline requests rather than holding large response buffers.
output reaches the consumer within 50-200 ms of the first generated token, compared to 8-30 s for a full blocking response.
users see text appearing continuously, which dramatically reduces the perception of latency even when total generation time is unchanged.
in agentic loops, tool calls can be detected and dispatched as soon as their arguments are streamed, before the LLM finishes generating the full response.
downstream consumers can begin processing partial results (summarization, routing decisions, UI rendering) in parallel with generation still in progress.
the full response never needs to be buffered in RAM before being forwarded — each chunk is processed and released.
if the agent detects from early tokens that the generation is heading in the wrong direction, it can abort the request and save the remaining generation cost.
the consumer must assemble an incomplete response incrementally. Tool-call argument accumulation, JSON parsing from partial text, and multi-block handling all require explicit state machines.
if a network error occurs halfway through a streamed response, there is no standard resume mechanism. The application must restart from scratch or rely on application-layer checkpointing.
Not suitable for all agentic steps:
steps whose outputs are used purely as inputs to later steps gain little from streaming if the downstream step must wait for the full output before it can proceed.
some HTTP reverse proxies (NGINX, AWS API Gateway) buffer responses by default, negating the streaming benefit. The proxy must be explicitly configured for streaming pass-through.
final token counts arrive in the last chunk (
). Billing and rate-limit logic must buffer for this final event.
if the consumer is slower than the producer (e.g., writing to a database after each chunk), explicit flow control is needed to avoid runaway memory growth.
When you call an LLM API with
, the server opens a long-lived HTTP connection and writes chunks of data as tokens are generated. The Anthropic API uses
(SSE), sending a sequence of typed events:
— the outer message object with model, role, and initial usage fields.
— the start of a content block (
— completion markers, with final usage in
The Anthropic Python SDK provides two levels of abstraction: the low-level
context manager (which exposes every raw event) and the high-level
iterator (which yields only the text deltas). For agentic use, the low-level interface is essential because tool-call events require access to block-level events, not just text.

attrs.alt: Streaming architecture: blocking vs streaming comparison, pipeline from agent through API gateway and LLM inference to stream handler and UI, plus agentic patterns

content.text: The pipeline shows how tokens flow from the LLM inference engine through the token stream (SSE / chunked HTTP) to a stream handler that routes, accumulates, and dispatches tool calls, then onwards to the UI or the next agent step. The four agentic patterns — tool-call streaming, multi-step streaming, fan-out streaming, and backpressure control — each address a distinct layer of the agentic loop.
always stream. Waiting 10+ seconds for a silent response destroys user trust regardless of response quality.
events. Dispatch the tool call as soon as its arguments are complete, without waiting for the rest of the message.
document drafting, code generation, and report writing all benefit from streaming because the user can begin reading and redirect the agent early if the output is going astray.
when SLA requires sub-second time-to-first-byte, streaming is the only mechanism that achieves it at LLM inference speeds.
the consumer is a pure data pipeline step (e.g., embedding, classification) that must see the complete response before it can act, and no human is waiting for visual feedback.
. The SDK handles SSE parsing, retry on connection drops, and provides both sync and async clients.
for full event access (tool calls, usage), or
to prepare for a tool call.
strings into a buffer, then JSON-parse the complete argument string when
for the assembled message including input token counts.
push each delta to the WebSocket or SSE connection immediately using
wrap the event loop in an
blocks automatically, applying backpressure to the LLM connection read.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# stream=True returns a MessageStreamManager (sync) or AsyncMessageStreamManager (async).
# Use as a context manager to ensure the connection is closed on exit.
with client.messages.stream(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Write a short essay on distributed systems."}],
) as stream:
    for text in stream.text_stream:
        # Each `text` is a small string fragment (often 1-4 tokens).
        # Print without a newline so tokens appear inline as they arrive.
        print(text, end="", flush=True)

print()  # final newline

# After the context manager exits, the full assembled message is available:
final_message = stream.get_final_message()
print(f"\nInput tokens: {final_message.usage.input_tokens}")
print(f"Output tokens: {final_message.usage.output_tokens}")

import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

tools = [{
    "name": "search_web",
    "description": "Search the web for current information.",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {"query": {"type": "string", "description": "The search query"}},
        "required": ["query"]
    }
}]

# Track streaming state per content block
current_tool_name: str | None = None
tool_args_buffer: str = ""
text_buffer: str = ""

with client.messages.stream(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=tools,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What's the latest news about AI regulation?"}],
) as stream:
    for event in stream:
        if event.type == "content_block_start":
            block = event.content_block
            if block.type == "tool_use":
                current_tool_name = block.name
                tool_args_buffer = ""
                print(f"\n[TOOL CALL STARTING: {block.name}]")

        elif event.type == "content_block_delta":
            delta = event.delta
            if delta.type == "text_delta":
                print(delta.text, end="", flush=True)
                text_buffer += delta.text
            elif delta.type == "input_json_delta":
                # Accumulate partial JSON -- not valid JSON until content_block_stop
                tool_args_buffer += delta.partial_json

        elif event.type == "content_block_stop":
            if current_tool_name:
                # Now the full JSON is assembled -- parse and dispatch
                args = json.loads(tool_args_buffer)
                print(f"\n[DISPATCHING {current_tool_name}({args})]")
                # In a real agent loop: result = await execute_tool(current_tool_name, args)
                current_tool_name = None
                tool_args_buffer = ""

message = stream.get_final_message()
print(f"\nStop reason: {message.stop_reason}")
Async streaming with FastAPI SSE endpoint

import anthropic, json
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse

client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()
app = FastAPI()

async def generate_stream(prompt: str):
    """Async generator that yields SSE-formatted chunks."""
    async with client.messages.stream(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=2048,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    ) as stream:
        async for text in stream.text_stream:
            # SSE format: each chunk is 'data: <json>\n\n'
            payload = json.dumps({"delta": text})
            yield f"data: {payload}\n\n"

        # Signal completion
        final = await stream.get_final_message()
        done_payload = json.dumps({
            "done": True,
            "usage": {
                "input_tokens": final.usage.input_tokens,
                "output_tokens": final.usage.output_tokens,
            }
        })
        yield f"data: {done_payload}\n\n"

@app.get("/stream")
async def stream_endpoint(q: str):
    return StreamingResponse(
        generate_stream(q),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={
            # Critical: disable proxy buffering (nginx, AWS ALB, etc.)
            "X-Accel-Buffering": "no",
            "Cache-Control": "no-cache",
        }
    )

A customer support agent receives user questions, may call internal knowledge-base tools, and streams the response back to the user in a web chat UI. The agent must dispatch tool calls as soon as their arguments are complete, not wait for the model to stop generating.
Buffering the full stream before forwarding:
before iterating events, or joining all chunks before sending to the UI, defeats the entire purpose of streaming.
on each delta will raise a
NGINX buffers responses by default. Without
, the entire stream is buffered at the proxy before the client receives anything.
context manager — or breaking out of it early without cleanup — can leave HTTP connections open indefinitely.
is truncated. Treating the accumulated text as a complete response will silently produce incorrect outputs downstream.
if writing to a buffered output, not calling
means chunks accumulate in the I/O buffer and are released in bursts, eliminating the streaming benefit.
dispatching a slow synchronous tool inside an async event loop blocks the loop, stalling the stream. All tool calls in async streaming agents must be awaitable.
Always use the async client in async contexts:
ensures the event loop is never blocked. The sync client wraps an internal thread pool that degrades at concurrency.
Prefer event-level iteration for tool-using agents:
gives access to all block types.
is convenient but filters out tool-call and thinking events.
SSE connections can be silently dropped by proxies after 30-60 seconds of inactivity. Send a
SSE comment every 15 seconds if you expect long generation times.
write chunks to the UI immediately; write to the database in a background task after the stream completes. Mixing blocking I/O into the streaming hot path adds unpredictable latency.
Set a hard timeout on the stream:
. Without a timeout, a stalled generation will hold the connection open indefinitely.
as a key metric. Regressions here signal infrastructure issues (cold starts, proxy misconfiguration, rate limiting).
generation time — the LLM generates the same number of tokens at the same speed. The performance gains are in
time from request sent to first
received. Typically 50-300 ms on Anthropic's API. A spike indicates rate limiting or cold start.
. Dropping throughput under load indicates the LLM backend is saturated.
each streaming request holds an HTTP connection for its full duration. Size the connection pool accordingly —
is a reasonable ceiling for a single process.
if each user request spawns N parallel LLM streams, your connection pool must support
simultaneous connections. Model this explicitly before scaling.
For scalable streaming architectures, consider routing streams through a dedicated
separate from your main API servers. Tools like Redis Streams or Kafka can fan out a single LLM stream to multiple consumers (UI, logger, analyzer) without re-calling the model.
Native async generator support; add X-Accel-Buffering: no header
Used internally by the Anthropic SDK; exposes iter_lines() for raw SSE parsing
astream_events() for per-step streaming; integrates with Claude via ChatAnthropic
Normalizes streaming events across Claude, OpenAI, etc.; adds caching + rate limiting
Must disable buffering: proxy_buffering off; (NGINX) or flush_interval 0 (Caddy)
Write each token chunk to a stream; multiple consumers read independently
Track TTFB histogram, tokens/sec by model, open connection count
Self-hosted inference; not available via API
Long system prompts reused across turns
Multi-step pipelines where steps are independent
Interactive agents needing mid-stream user input
is truncated. Acting on a truncated tool-call response can produce silent data corruption.
streaming HTML or Markdown directly into the DOM without escaping opens XSS vectors. Sanitize incrementally or accumulate and sanitize before render.
never dispatch a tool call based on partial
and validate the fully assembled JSON against the tool schema before executing.
around the entire stream. A stalled connection that never sends
will hold resources indefinitely without a timeout.
each open SSE connection consumes a file descriptor and a TCP socket. Without per-user concurrency limits, a malicious client can open thousands of idle streams, exhausting server resources.
avoid logging full streamed payloads to persistent storage unless necessary — they may contain sensitive user data. Log token counts, request IDs, and latency metrics instead.
Streaming reduces time-to-first-byte from the full generation time (8-30 s) to the time of the first token (50-200 ms), without changing total generation cost or throughput.
only when you need text output and no tool events.
fragments; accumulate them fully and parse JSON only after
Proxy buffering (NGINX, API Gateway) silently negates streaming benefits — always configure
and verify TTFB end-to-end, not just at the application layer.
In agentic loops, streaming enables early tool dispatch — tools can be called as soon as their arguments are assembled, cutting multi-step pipeline latency significantly.
Always set a hard timeout on the stream, validate
before acting on the output, and sanitize streamed text before rendering to avoid security vulnerabilities.

Combine streaming with prompt caching and parallel fan-out for compounding latency reductions: caching cuts TTFB on repeated prefixes; fan-out reduces wall-clock time across multi-step pipelines; streaming delivers the first token of each step instantly.
